quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Two-way table"?

options.text: Increasing n decreases the margin of error proportionally to 1/√n, producing a narrower (more precise) interval
A table displaying observed counts for every combination of two categorical variables, with row totals, column totals, and grand total
SD(X ± Y) = √(Var(X) + Var(Y)) for independent random variables; note that SDs do NOT simply add
The sampling distribution of p̂ is approximately normal when np ≥ 10 AND n(1-p) ≥ 10 (Large Counts condition)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Two-way table" is defined as: A table displaying observed counts for every combination of two categorical variables, with row totals, column totals, and grand total. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Association in two-way tables"?

options.text: Tests H₀ that a categorical variable follows a specified distribution by comparing observed and expected counts
Retrospective studies look backward at existing data; prospective studies follow subjects forward in time from the start
Two variables are associated if conditional distributions of one variable differ across categories of the other
The probability of obtaining a test statistic as extreme as or more extreme than the observed value, assuming H₀ is true

quiz_questions.explanation: "Association in two-way tables" is defined as: Two variables are associated if conditional distributions of one variable differ across categories of the other. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Segmented bar chart"?

options.text: A graphical display of conditional distributions; bars divided into segments showing the proportion in each category of the response variable
df = k - 1, where k is the number of categories; determines the shape of the chi-square distribution
The probability of correctly rejecting H₀ when Hₐ is true; Power = 1 - β; increased by larger n, larger effect size, or larger α
A statistically significant slope may have little practical importance if the effect is very small; always consider R² and the magnitude of b₁

quiz_questions.explanation: "Segmented bar chart" is defined as: A graphical display of conditional distributions; bars divided into segments showing the proportion in each category of the response variable. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the context of Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions, which best defines "Marginal distribution"?

options.text: Tests H₀: μ = μ₀ using t = (x̄ - μ₀)/(s/√n) with df = n - 1; used when σ is unknown
df = k - 1, where k is the number of categories; determines the shape of the chi-square distribution
The distribution of one variable ignoring the other; calculated from the row or column totals divided by the grand total
P(not A) = 1 - P(A); the probability that an event does NOT occur equals one minus the probability it does occur

quiz_questions.explanation: In Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions, "Marginal distribution" refers to: The distribution of one variable ignoring the other; calculated from the row or column totals divided by the grand total.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the context of Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions, which best defines "Conditional distribution"?

options.text: (O - E)/√E for each cell; values beyond ±2 indicate that cell contributes notably to the overall χ² statistic
The distribution of one variable within a specific category of the other; cell counts divided by the appropriate row or column total
A smooth curve that models the overall shape of a distribution; the total area under the curve equals 1 and represents probability
Independence: one random sample classified by two variables; Homogeneity: separate random samples from each population

quiz_questions.explanation: In Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions, "Conditional distribution" refers to: The distribution of one variable within a specific category of the other; cell counts divided by the appropriate row or column total.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A table displaying observed counts for every combination of two categorical variables, with row totals, column totals, and grand total"?

options.text: Multiplication rule for independents
LINE conditions
False positive/negative
Two-way table

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Two-way table".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Two variables are associated if conditional distributions of one variable differ across categories of the other"?

options.text: Degrees of freedom (df)
Significance level (α)
Association in two-way tables
Pooled proportion

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Association in two-way tables".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A graphical display of conditional distributions; bars divided into segments showing the proportion in each category of the response variable"?

options.text: Response bias
Segmented bar chart
Standardized residual
Z-score

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Segmented bar chart".

quiz_questions.question_text: In Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions, which term matches: "The distribution of one variable ignoring the other; calculated from the row or column totals divided by the grand total"?

options.text: Residual plot
Placebo
Simpson's Paradox
Marginal distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: In Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions, this refers to "Marginal distribution".

quiz_questions.question_text: In Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions, which term matches: "The distribution of one variable within a specific category of the other; cell counts divided by the appropriate row or column total"?

options.text: Zero in the interval
Two-proportion z-test
Standard normal distribution
Conditional distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: In Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions, this refers to "Conditional distribution".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions is accurate?

options.text: Conditional Probability measures the probability of an event given that another event has occurred: P(A|B) = P(A and B)/P(B).
A significant result indicates that the observed data are unlikely under H₀ but does not identify which specific categories differ or quantify the strength of association.
The slope b = r(sy/sx) represents the predicted change in y for a one-unit increase in x, while the y-intercept a gives the predicted y when x = 0.
Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions organize data for two categorical variables and provide the foundation for chi-square analysis.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions?

options.text: Marginal distributions describe each variable alone (row or column totals divided by the grand total), while conditional distributions show the distribution of one variable within levels of the other.
Binomial and Geometric Distributions model two important types of count data.
Conditional Probability measures the probability of an event given that another event has occurred: P(A|B) = P(A and B)/P(B).
Interpreting Chi-Square Results requires understanding what a significant χ² value means and what it does not.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions?

options.text: For any random variables: E(X ± Y) = E(X) ± E(Y), always.
Comparing conditional distributions across rows or columns reveals whether the variables are associated.
A randomized block design combines the benefits of both techniques: blocking controls for known confounders while randomization balances unknown confounders across treatment groups.
The p-value measures the probability of observing data as extreme as or more extreme than the sample result, assuming H₀ is true.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions?

options.text: Understanding this distinction is critical—many important questions (smoking's effect on health, educational interventions) rely on observational data when experiments are unethical or impractical.

The three major types—selection bias, response bias, and nonresponse bias—must be addressed through careful study design, while confounding variables create false associations that mask or mimic true causal relationships.
Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions organize data for two categorical variables and provide the foundation for chi-square analysis.
Residuals and Least-Squares Regression fit a linear model ŷ = a + bx to bivariate data by minimizing the sum of squared residuals (Σ(y - ŷ)²).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions?

options.text: Interpreting Chi-Square Results requires understanding what a significant χ² value means and what it does not.

Marginal distributions describe each variable alone (row or column totals divided by the grand total), while conditional distributions show the distribution of one variable within levels of the other.

Probability quantifies uncertainty on a scale from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain), with the Law of Large Numbers stating that relative frequency approaches true probability as the number of trials increases.
Comparing Two Proportions tests whether two population proportions differ using the test statistic z = (p̂₁ - p̂₂)/√(p̂c(1-p̂c)(1/n₁ + 1/n₂)), where p̂c is the pooled proportion under H₀: p₁ = p₂.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions?

options.text: Quality assurance using systematic sampling (every 50th item on an assembly line) to efficiently monitor production consistency
Analyzing survey data on exercise frequency (daily, weekly, rarely) conditioned on age group (18-30, 31-50, 51+) to identify demographic health patterns
Political polling organizations using stratified sampling by demographic groups (age, region, party) to produce accurate election forecasts
Evaluating whether a new teaching method causes improved test scores requires an experiment with random assignment—simply comparing schools using different methods is observational

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions?

options.text: Market research using two-way tables to determine whether product preference varies by age group for targeted advertising strategies
Polling organizations reporting margin of error, which depends on the standard error σ/√n, explaining why larger polls are more precise
Hospital data examining whether survival rates (survived/died) are conditionally distributed differently across different treatment regimens
Testing whether a coin is fair (H₀: p = 0.5) by flipping it 100 times and computing the z-statistic from the observed proportion of heads

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Social media analysis estimating the proportion of posts containing misinformation and computing a confidence interval using the normal approximation to p̂
Laboratory experiments controlling temperature, humidity, and light exposure when testing plant growth under different fertilizer treatments
College admissions data examining acceptance rates conditional on gender to investigate potential bias, while being alert for Simpson's Paradox across departments
Modeling customer arrival times at a store using appropriate probability distributions to optimize staffing schedules

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions?

options.text: Analyzing survey data on exercise frequency (daily, weekly, rarely) conditioned on age group (18-30, 31-50, 51+) to identify demographic health patterns
Clinical trials comparing cure rates between drug and placebo groups to determine whether the drug is more effective than no treatment
Testing whether a coin is fair (H₀: p = 0.5) by flipping it 100 times and computing the z-statistic from the observed proportion of heads
Healthcare studies using cluster sampling of hospitals rather than individual patients when a complete patient list is impractical

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions in practice?

options.text: Clinical trials comparing cure rates between drug and placebo groups to determine whether the drug is more effective than no treatment
Medical researchers estimating the rate at which blood pressure changes per year of age, with a confidence interval quantifying the uncertainty in the estimate
A/B testing at tech companies: testing whether a new feature increases click-through rate, with H₀: no difference and p-value determining rollout decisions
Hospital data examining whether survival rates (survived/died) are conditionally distributed differently across different treatment regimens

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions.
